Visit Kalighat Temple, one of Hinduism's 51 Shakti Peethas, dedicated to goddess Kali - indeed, Kali gave Calcutta its name. The current temple dates from 1809, but the site has been venerated for centuries. The atmosphere is intense: lines of devotees, flower and incense offerings, priests in trance. Non-Hindus are generally accepted in the outer courtyard.

Head to College Street, the world's largest concentration of bookstores after Buenos Aires. Sidewalks disappear under piles of books - university textbooks, Bengali novels, Tagore poetry, and all sorts of rarities. Coffee House café, an institution since 1942, was Bengali intellectuals' meeting place - Satyajit Ray, Amartya Sen, and Subhas Chandra Bose all hung out here.

activities.text: Visit Mother Teresa's House (Missionaries of Charity), where the saint lived and worked from 1953 until her death in 1997. Her tomb lies in the ground floor chapel. A small museum traces her life and work among Kolkata's poorest. Entry is free and the place is imbued with moving simplicity.

Take a boat ride on the Hooghly (Ganges branch) at sunset. From the water, you'll see the illuminated Howrah Bridge pass by, Kolkata's ghats, and old colonial mansions lining the river. Several operators offer one-hour cruises from Prinsep ghats.

End the evening with a Bengali classical dance or music performance - Kolkata is India's cultural capital, birthplace of Satyajit Ray's cinema and Ravi Shankar's music. Nandan and Rabindra Sadan halls regularly program events.

visits.text: Train from Kolkata to Bhubaneswar (about 7 hours on the Shatabdi). The 'city of a thousand temples' closes this circuit with a return to Indian spirituality's roots. Lingaraj Temple, Bhubaneswar's largest and most venerated, dates from the 11th century. Its 55-meter tower (deul) dominates the city - it's the finest example of Kalinga architectural style. Entry is reserved for Hindus, but you can observe the temple from a raised platform outside the enclosure, originally installed for Lord Curzon.

Rajarani Temple (11th century) is nicknamed the 'love temple' for its sculptures of entwined couples and celestial nymphs recalling Khajuraho, on a smaller scale. Unlike Lingaraj, it's open to all. The surrounding garden is peaceful - the golden sandstone sculptures in late afternoon light are magnificent.

Visit Udayagiri and Khandagiri caves, carved in the 2nd century BC by King Kharavela to house Jain monks. Udayagiri ('sunrise hill') has 18 caves adorned with detailed bas-reliefs showing court scenes and elephant processions. Rani Gumpha ('queen's cave'), on two levels, is the most spectacular. Khandagiri, opposite, offers a panoramic view over Bhubaneswar from its summit.

If you have time, detour to Dhauli (8km south), where Emperor Ashoka, after the bloody Battle of Kalinga in 261 BC that killed over 100,000, had a crisis of conscience that converted him to Buddhism. The rock edicts of Ashoka carved in stone are among India's oldest texts. The white Shanti Stupa (Peace Stupa), built in 1972 by the Japanese at the hilltop, offers clear views over rice paddies.

activities.text: Explore Ekamra Haat market, a permanent craft market where Odisha artisans display their specialties: stone carvings, patachitra (cloth paintings), Cuttack silver filigree jewelry, and ikat fabrics. It's the ideal spot for quality souvenirs at reasonable prices.

Taste Odisha cuisine: dalma (lentils with vegetables and coconut), pakhala bhata (rice fermented in water - Odisha's emblematic dish, served cold), and chhena poda (caramelized fresh cheese cake), a specialty unavailable elsewhere in India.

seo.h1_title: Grand Northern India Circuit: Delhi, Varanasi & the Himalayas
seo.intro_paragraph: From the frenzy of Old Delhi's bazaars to Manali's eternal snows, from the Golden Temple's golden prayers in Amritsar to Varanasi's sacred pyres - this 19-day circuit crosses North India in all its power. You'll go from Mughal palaces at the Taj Mahal to Khajuraho's erotic temples, from Rishikesh ashrams to the very place of Buddha's Enlightenment at Bodhgaya, ending in intellectual Kolkata and Bhubaneswar of a thousand temples.
seo.seo_hook: Taj Mahal at dawn, yoga by the Ganges, Himalayas from Manali, Varanasi ghats at twilight - North India concentrates the country's most powerful images.
featured_list: Delhi: Mughal chaos and colonial grandeur between Red Fort and Qutub Minar
Amritsar: the Golden Temple reflects in Sikhism's sacred pool
Varanasi: thousand-year-old ghats burn day and night on the Ganges banks
Khajuraho: 25 tenth-century temples carved with 900 divine and erotic figures

places_taglines.IN::delhi: Mughal and colonial capital where 3,000 years of history intersect
places_taglines.IN::amritsar: the Golden Temple shines at the heart of Sikh faith
places_taglines.IN::dharamsala: Himalayan little Lhasa, the Dalai Lama's refuge since 1959
places_taglines.IN::manali: mountain resort nestled in Kullu valley facing the peaks
places_taglines.IN::chandigarh: city planned by Le Corbusier, UNESCO-listed in 2016
places_taglines.IN::rishikesh: world capital of yoga perched at the Himalayas' foot on the Ganges
places_taglines.IN::haridwar: holy city where the Ganges enters the northern plains
places_taglines.IN::agra: the white marble Taj Mahal watches over the Yamuna since 1653
places_taglines.IN::orchha: forgotten Bundela city where palaces mirror in the Betwa
places_taglines.IN::khajuraho: 25 tenth-century temples adorned with divine and sensual sculptures
places_taglines.IN::varanasi: the world's oldest city burns and prays beside the Ganges
places_taglines.IN::bodhgaya: under the Bodhi tree Buddha attained Enlightenment
places_taglines.IN::kolkata: former Raj capital between colonial splendor and cultural effervescence
places_taglines.IN::bhubaneswar: the city of a thousand temples preserves Kalinga architecture

highlights: The Taj Mahal at dawn when mist rises over the Yamuna - 22 years of construction, 20,000 workers
Ganga Aarti ceremony in Varanasi with 7 priests and hundreds of floating lamps on the Ganges
Amritsar's Golden Temple covered with 750kg of pure gold and its free langar for 50,000 people daily
Khajuraho temples: 900 sculptures on a single temple of which only 10% are erotic
